deaRegistrationStatus.headline: DEA Registration Status — Oklahoma Operators

tracks.status: Portal LIVE — likely closing June 22 (per MJBizDaily/Fennemore Craig); optional per OMMA
tracks.detail: The DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov. Fee $794/yr (PayPal only). OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries — non-registrants may continue operating under Oklahoma state law. CRITICAL UPDATE — PORTAL CLOSING DATE: Multiple credible sources including MJBizDaily (June 5, 2026) and law firm Fennemore Craig cite June 22, 2026 as the dispensary portal's own hard closing date — 5 days before the official 60-day Federal Register window (June 27). This has not been confirmed in a standalone official DEA press release, but dispensaries should treat June 22 as the effective filing deadline and act immediately — 8 days remain as of June 14. Vertically integrated operators that also hold a manufacturer/distributor license are still subject to the OBNDD requirement below.

sources.name: OMMA — DEA application instructions for dispensaries
MJBizDaily — DEA registration is a tough choice (June 5, 2026)

tracks.status: No dedicated portal yet — OBNDD says required — Form 225 is only current path
tracks.detail: As of 2026-06-14 the dedicated DEA application forms for Medical Marijuana Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor are NOT yet live. A DEA Diversion Control notice (~May 11, 2026) said forms are 'coming in the coming weeks' — now nearly seven weeks later with the expedited window closing in approximately 12–13 days (June 26–27), no dedicated portal has launched and it is effectively certain one will not launch before the deadline. DEA Form 225 with medical-marijuana drug codes 7362 / 7353 / 7386 is the ONLY viable pathway to meet the expedited deadline. Do not wait. Fees under Form 225: $3,699/yr (Manufacturer), $1,850/yr (Distributor), $296/yr (Analytical Lab). CRITICAL: DEA has confirmed that operators who apply via Form 225 before the dedicated portal launches do NOT need to reapply — DEA will contact them to continue processing. OBNDD (May 8, 2026) states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to obtain DEA registration; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027.

deaRegistrationStatus.expeditedWindow: 60-day expedited window closes ~June 26–27, 2026 (60 days from the April 28, 2026 Federal Register publication; June 26 is the last business day; June 27 is the last calendar day). DISPENSARY PORTAL CLOSING EARLIER: Multiple credible sources including MJBizDaily (June 5) and Fennemore Craig now cite June 22 as the date the dispensary portal itself closes — 5 days before the 60-day window. If accurate, dispensaries have 8 days remaining as of June 14 and should file immediately. Whether Form 225 serves as a fallback for dispensaries after June 22 is unclear; do not rely on it. For manufacturers/cultivators the 'June 22' deadline remains WRONG — June 26–27 is the correct window for Form 225 filers. The original explanation that June 22 is the ALJ hearing participant notification date (from the same April 28 Federal Register document) also remains accurate for that separate purpose. D.C. Circuit briefing: SAM/NDASA filed their opening brief June 4; Nebraska/Indiana opening brief expected ~June 26; government (DOJ) response to Nebraska/Indiana petition due July 2. No stay has been granted as of June 14, 2026. The NDASA/MMJ stay motion (filed June 9) remains pending — no court ruling has issued as of June 14.
deaRegistrationStatus.caution: Multiple consultants and vendors are marketing paid 'DEA registration' services, some implying guaranteed or expedited approval. The portal is a direct-to-applicant system; the DEA makes all decisions and cites a six-month review period. For manufacturers/cultivators: 'June 22' as a general registration deadline is a red flag — the correct date is June 26–27 via Form 225. For dispensaries: June 22 is now cited by multiple credible sources as the portal closing date — treat it as the operative deadline. The application's Section 4 self-incrimination question warrants independent legal counsel before any owner/officer answers and files. Registration grants DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority — operators should assess this exposure with counsel. UNVETTED SOURCES: Content-marketing websites with no editorial credentials (HealthDataConsortium.org, WeedPress, hrzenden.com/Zen Den Co.) are appearing in search results alongside legitimate law-firm guidance — verify all compliance information against .gov primary sources only.

officialSources.name: Federal Register — Rescheduling of Marijuana (state-licensed medical)
OMMA — monitoring DEA status & SAM lawsuit

keyDeadlines.label: Dispensary DEA Portal Likely Closes — June 22, 2026
keyDeadlines.description: Multiple credible sources including MJBizDaily (June 5, 2026) and law firm Fennemore Craig cite June 22, 2026 as the closing date of the DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov — 5 days before the official 60-day Federal Register window (June 27). OMMA states dispensary registration is optional, but dispensaries choosing to register must act by June 22. 8 days remain as of June 14. Not confirmed in a standalone official DEA press release; treat June 22 as the operative deadline.

keyDeadlines.label: DEA Expedited 60-Day Registration Window
keyDeadlines.description: DEA expedited 60-day registration window closes. Apply within 60 days of the April 28 Federal Register publication to keep operating during the six-month DEA review. Only 12–13 days remain as of June 14. For cultivators, manufacturers, labs, and distributors: the dedicated DEA portal has NOT launched — use DEA Form 225 with drug codes 7362/7353/7386 NOW. Do not wait for the dedicated portal.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Deadline — OMMA Manufacturers & Distributors
keyDeadlines.description: OBNDD deadline for OMMA manufacturers & distributors to file for DEA registration. Failure may result in OBNDD administrative sanctions including revocation of the OBNDD registration that underpins OMMA grower licenses. Use DEA Form 225 with medical marijuana drug codes — the dedicated manufacturer portal has not yet launched.

keyDeadlines.label: D.C. Circuit — Nebraska/Indiana Brief; Govt Response July 2
keyDeadlines.description: Nebraska and Indiana AGs' opening brief expected ~June 26 in D.C. Circuit consolidated cases (26-1106/1130/1136), challenging the DEA rescheduling order. SAM/NDASA filed their opening brief June 4. Government (DOJ) response to Nebraska/Indiana petition due July 2. A court ruling granting a stay would revert marijuana to Schedule I and nullify registrations filed under the expedited pathway. NDASA/MMJ stay motion (filed June 9) remains pending as of June 14.

keyDeadlines.label: Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing
keyDeadlines.description: Broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing (DEA Arlington, VA). Determines whether adult-use marijuana also moves to Schedule III. July 3 recess included. Hearing faces a pre-hearing constitutional challenge over the DEA ALJ structure. No pre-hearing stay has been granted as of June 14, 2026; hearing remains on schedule.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma New-License Moratorium Expiration (Extended to Aug 2028)
keyDeadlines.description: Oklahoma HB 3143 was signed into law by Governor Stitt on May 7, 2026, extending the new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028. No new cultivation, processing, dispensary, or transport licenses will be issued until that date. Companion HB 3144 (2,500-license cultivation cap) passed the House; its enactment status is pending confirmation.

keyDeadlines.label: Federal Hemp Redefinition Takes Effect
keyDeadlines.description: Federal hemp redefinition takes effect: 0.3% total-THC threshold (includes THCA); final-product cap 0.4 mg THC; synthetics excluded. Delta-8 / Delta-10 / THCA-flower market effectively eliminated unless reformulated.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Enforcement Grace Period Ends
keyDeadlines.description: OBNDD enforcement grace period ends. Oklahoma operators without DEA registration by this date face active OBNDD administrative sanctions, regardless of whether they applied before the June 27 window.

items.headline: DEA Reschedules State Medical Marijuana & FDA-Approved Products to Schedule III
items.summary: Acting AG Todd Blanche issued a final order moving (1) marijuana in FDA-approved drug products and (2) marijuana under a qualifying state medical license from Schedule I to Schedule III, using treaty-implementation authority (21 U.S.C. 811(d)(1)) without traditional notice-and-comment. Recreational/adult-use, unlicensed marijuana, and synthetically derived THC remain Schedule I. A parallel expedited rulemaking on broader rescheduling was simultaneously initiated.
items.whyItMatters: Most consequential federal cannabis action in decades — creates new DEA registration duties, 280E tax relief, and treaty-compliance obligations for thousands of operators.

items.headline: DEA Registration Now Required — Expedited Window Closes ~June 26–27, 2026
items.summary: Dispensary portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov ($794/yr, PayPal only) — OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries; multiple credible sources including MJBizDaily cite June 22 as the portal's own closing date, 5 days before the 60-day Federal Register window. Dedicated DEA application forms for Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor are NOT yet live as of 2026-06-14; a DEA Diversion Control notice (~May 11) said forms are 'coming in the coming weeks,' and nearly seven weeks later no dedicated portal has launched. The parallel pathway is DEA Form 225 with codes 7362/7353/7386 (fees: $3,699/yr manufacturer; $1,850/yr distributor; $296/yr lab). OBNDD states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to register; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027. The expedited 60-day window closes ~June 26–27 (60 days from April 28 Federal Register publication).
items.whyItMatters: Every cultivator, processor, lab, distributor, and dispensary faces a new federal registration layer. Dispensaries have a live portal but must act by June 22 per multiple credible sources. Manufacturers/cultivators must use Form 225 now — OBNDD says non-registration risks sanctions. Missing the expedited window means operating without safe-harbor during a six-month DEA review.

items.headline: OBNDD Mandates DEA Registration for OMMA Manufacturers & Distributors — Deadline ~June 27; Grace Period to Jan 1, 2027
items.summary: OBNDD Director Donnie Anderson directed that every OMMA registrant who is a "distributor or manufacturer" must obtain federal DEA registration. Applying within 60 days of the April 28 publication (~June 27, 2026) lets a business keep operating under state authority during DEA's six-month review. Anderson warned that failure "could result in OBNDD administrative sanctions up to and including the potential revocation" of OBNDD registrations, with an enforcement grace period running until January 1, 2027. The DEA application has seven sections and costs approximately $3,699 for manufacturers and $1,850 for distributors (via Form 225).
items.whyItMatters: OBNDD's requirement reaches ALL licensed manufacturers (growers) and distributors, not just dispensaries; growers who miss it risk losing the OBNDD manufacturer registration that underpins their OMMA grower license.

items.headline: Oklahoma Moratorium Extended to 2028 — HB 3143 Signed May 7; HB 3144 Cultivation Cap Pending Confirmation
items.summary: Governor Kevin Stitt signed HB 3143 into law on May 7, 2026, extending Oklahoma's new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028; the bill also prohibits transfer of any license currently under investigation. Companion HB 3144 — which would cap total cultivation licenses at 2,500 statewide — passed the House (82–15); its enactment status remains unconfirmed as of June 14, 2026. The Oklahoma legislature adjourned sine die on May 14, 2026, closing the 2026 session. At House passage Oklahoma had 2,164 active grows, 686 processors, 1,421 dispensaries, and 58 transporters. The moratorium applies only to new licenses; existing licensees in good standing may still open new locations or transfer licenses with OMMA approval.
items.whyItMatters: The two-year moratorium extension is now law — no new cultivation or other cannabis licenses will be issued until at least August 2028. Existing grow licenses become scarcer and more valuable as transferable assets. If HB 3144 was enacted before sine die adjournment, the 2,500 cap locks the total count near its current level.

items.headline: Schedule III + Single Convention Activates Five New Compliance Burdens for Cultivators
items.summary: Because the rescheduling order is grounded in treaty-implementation authority, it increases — not reduces — cultivator compliance burdens: (1) SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access; (2) disclosure of upstream supplier DEA numbers; (3) DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority; (4) a crop purchase-and-resale mechanism where DEA serves as exclusive purchaser at a nominal price before crops can leave the facility; (5) parallel federal recordkeeping including quota reporting and import/export permits. The 2026 cultivator administrative fee is $113/kg on the 6,675 kg aggregate quota.
items.whyItMatters: Operators who assumed "rescheduling = deregulation" face a compliance shock; the DEA purchase-resale mechanism restructures the cultivation supply chain.

items.headline: D.C. Circuit Cases 26-1106/1130/1136: SAM/NDASA Brief Filed June 4 — Nebraska/Indiana Brief Expected June 26; Govt Response July 2; Louisiana Withdrew
items.summary: Three consolidated petition sets challenge the April 28 order: (1) Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) + NDASA, filed May 4 — opening brief filed June 4; (2) AGs of Nebraska and Indiana, filed May 22 — opening brief expected ~June 26; government (DOJ) response to Nebraska/Indiana petition due July 2 (Louisiana initially co-filed but AG Liz Murrill sought dismissal May 29, leaving only Nebraska and Indiana); (3) a coalition of five 'aggrieved persons' including addiction-treatment organizations (New Directions) and others, filed May 28. Arguments include: exceeds AG authority, violates APA notice-and-comment, implicates the major questions doctrine, and relies on a possibly unconstitutional ALJ structure. All petitioner groups seek both a stay and full vacatur. On June 9, NDASA + MMJ International Holdings + MMJ BioPharma Cultivation + MMJ BioPharma Labs separately filed a formal stay motion in Case 26-1136. No stay has been granted as of June 14, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: A stay could freeze the entire order — reverting state medical marijuana to Schedule I and nullifying registration, 280E relief, and research expansion. The Nebraska/Indiana brief (~June 26) and government response (July 2) are the next immediate court milestones; any subsequent stay ruling is the key risk event for Oklahoma operators.

items.headline: NDASA + MMJ BioPharma File D.C. Circuit Stay Motion — First Formal Stay Request in Case 26-1136
items.summary: On June 9, 2026, the National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA), MMJ International Holdings, MMJ BioPharma Cultivation, and MMJ BioPharma Labs jointly filed a motion in D.C. Circuit Case No. 26-1136 seeking a stay of Attorney General Order No. 6754-2026 (the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order). The petitioners argue (1) the order bypassed required notice-and-comment rulemaking; (2) the AG lacked authority to act without completing the statutory DEA hearing; and (3) MMJ International/MMJ BioPharma — which invested more than $10 million and nearly a decade developing cannabinoid pharmaceuticals through FDA/DEA channels — suffer commercial harm from state-licensed marijuana operators now competing under the same Schedule III classification without equivalent safety review. This is a distinct stay motion from the June 4 SAM/NDASA opening brief. Petitioners may also seek emergency relief before the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing. No court ruling has issued as of June 14.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first formal motion in Case 26-1136 asking the D.C. Circuit to actually halt the order. A granted stay would revert state medical marijuana to Schedule I, nullify all DEA registrations filed under the expedited pathway, and undo 280E relief. Oklahoma operators filing DEA registration before the June 26–27 window preserves the safe-harbor regardless of litigation outcome; the risk of not registering (OBNDD sanctions, license jeopardy) is immediate and certain, while stay risk is real but not yet realized.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — NDASA/MMJ Ask Court to Pause Rescheduling

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — Drug Testing Industry Asks Court to Pause

items.headline: Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing Set for June 29 – July 15, 2026 — Faces Pre-Hearing Constitutional Challenge
items.summary: Alongside the partial rescheduling, DEA withdrew its stalled August 2024 proceeding and issued a new Notice of Hearing for an expedited ALJ proceeding on whether ALL marijuana — including adult-use — should move to Schedule III via notice-and-comment rulemaking. The hearing runs June 29 – July 15, 2026 (July 3 recess) at DEA's Arlington, VA facility. A pre-hearing constitutional challenge is active: the DOJ formally admitted in MMJ BioPharma Cultivation Inc. v. Bondi (Feb. 27, 2025) that DEA ALJ removal protections are unconstitutional; challengers argue that under Axon v. FTC (2022), subjecting parties to proceedings before an unlawful tribunal is itself irreparable injury. MMJ International announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the hearing; no such injunction has been granted and the hearing remains on schedule as of June 14, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: Determines whether recreational marijuana joins medical in Schedule III; any final rule would also be subject to a Congressional Review Act challenge. If the constitutional challenge succeeds in obtaining a pre-hearing stay, the broader-rescheduling process stalls indefinitely. The April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order is a separate instrument and is not automatically affected by the hearing's outcome.

secondarySource.name: Business of Cannabis — Mounting Legal Challenges

items.headline: 280E Tax Deduction Bar Lifted for Medical Operators — Binding IRS/Treasury Guidance Still Pending
items.summary: State-licensed medical marijuana is no longer Schedule I/II, so operators are no longer subject to the IRC Section 280E deduction disallowance. Treasury/IRS announced April 23, 2026 that formal guidance is forthcoming and expected to treat rescheduling as applying for the full 2026 tax year. As of June 14, 2026 — more than six weeks after the order took effect — no revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance has been published. Seven House Democrats formally demanded IRS and Treasury expedite guidance on May 28 (see companion item). No retroactive relief for prior years has been announced; recreational-only operators remain fully subject to 280E.
items.whyItMatters: Potentially tens of millions in aggregate annual tax savings for medical operators; however, operators and CPAs are acting on legal interpretation alone until IRS publishes binding guidance. Allocation methodologies for mixed-use operators and retroactivity scope remain unresolved.

items.headline: IRS/Treasury 280E Guidance Still Not Issued — Seven House Democrats Demand Action
items.summary: Seven House Democrats led by Reps. Steven Horsford and Steve Cohen wrote to IRS Commissioner Danny Bisignano and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on May 28, 2026, demanding prompt publication of formal 280E guidance following the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order. As of June 14, 2026 — more than six weeks after rescheduling took effect — neither IRS nor Treasury has published a revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance on how 280E applies to Schedule III medical-marijuana operators. The April 23 Treasury press release (SB0471) announced intent to issue guidance including a transition rule treating relief as applying for the full 2026 taxable year; it is not itself binding. Operators and CPAs are acting on the legal interpretation that 280E does not apply to state-licensed medical activity, but without IRS confirmation of allocation methodologies or treatment of mixed-use operators.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma medical-marijuana operators — cultivators, processors, and dispensaries — cannot finalize 2026 tax strategy with certainty until IRS publishes formal guidance. The six-week gap between the rescheduling order and any binding IRS action leaves operators exposed if Treasury ultimately limits relief in ways not currently anticipated.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — 7 House Democrats Demand 280E Guidance

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — Lawmakers Urge IRS and Treasury

items.headline: Congress Closes the Hemp Loophole — Delta-8 / THCA / Synthetics Banned Effective Nov 12, 2026
items.summary: The Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2026 (H.R. 5371 §781), enacted November 12, 2025, rewrote the federal hemp definition: the 0.3% THC threshold now counts total THC including THCA; final consumable products are capped at 0.4 mg total THC per container; intermediates are capped at 0.3% total THC dry-weight post-decarboxylation; cannabinoids not naturally produced by the plant or synthesized outside it are excluded and treated as Schedule I. Products exceeding thresholds after November 12, 2026 revert to Schedule I.
items.whyItMatters: Functionally eliminates the current Delta-8/Delta-10/THCA-flower intoxicating-hemp market unless reformulated or Congress acts before the effective date.

items.headline: OMMA Monitors Rescheduling & Confirms 280E Relief Applies Regardless of DEA Registration Status
items.summary: OMMA's official notice confirms it is monitoring DEA rescheduling, that approximately 4,000 OMMA licensees are affected, and that 280E removal applies to Oklahoma medical operators regardless of whether a licensee pursues DEA registration. OMMA cautioned that all information "remains subject to change pending petition outcomes" — a reference to the consolidated D.C. Circuit litigation.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma operators get federal tax relief now, but the pending lawsuits are the key wildcard that could reverse it.

items.headline: OMMA Enforcement: 6,000+ Inspections, 1,400+ Administrative Cases, 704 Products Recalled
items.summary: OMMA reported 6,000+ compliance inspections, 1,400+ administrative cases, and 704 products recalled through June 2024. A February 17, 2026 update reported 17 more administrative actions in three weeks, embargoing thousands of unregulated plants and products. Enforcement targets include secret-shopping 50 dispensaries and testing 100+ samples per month at OMMA's QA Reference Lab.
items.whyItMatters: OMMA is in an active enforcement posture; growers and processors should treat Metrc accuracy, labeling, and credentialing as live enforcement priorities, not administrative formalities.

items.headline: Oklahoma Grower License: Current Compliance Stack — Metrc, OBNDD Registration, 75% Residency, $50K Bond
items.summary: Commercial growers must comply with OMMA rules and hold an OBNDD "Manufacturer" registration. Key requirements: ≥75% Oklahoma-resident ownership; owners ≥25 years old; background checks (nonviolent felony within 2 yrs / any felony within 5 yrs disqualifying); $50,000 surety bond or 5+ years land ownership; not within 1,000 ft of a school; outdoor growers register with the Oklahoma Department of Agriculture; active Metrc seed-to-sale tracking (tags at vegetative stage, movements reported within 24 hrs, sales pushed to Metrc same day). Tier-1 fees range $2,558.30 to $50,000+ by canopy size.
items.whyItMatters: The 75% residency rule is the main anti-foreign-ownership mechanism; post-moratorium applicants will face all of this plus the new DEA registration layer.

items.headline: Illegal-Grow Crackdown: 51-Defendant Federal Indictment; 1.7M+ Plants Seized Since 2021
items.summary: A multi-agency task force (Oklahoma AG Organized Crime Task Force, OBN/OBNDD, OMMA, ICE/HSI, Cherokee Nation Marshals, National Guard) targets illegal grows, many linked to Chinese nationals. A June 24, 2025 Craig/Mayes County operation seized 40,723 plants and 1,000+ lbs of processed marijuana. In April 2026 a DEA-led Homeland Security Task Force announced a 51-defendant federal indictment over a nationwide black-market conspiracy originating in Oklahoma. OBNDD Director Anderson testified that product from ~85 million plants is unaccounted for. Since 2021: 1.7 million illegal plants seized, 181,000 lbs confiscated, 302 arrests.
items.whyItMatters: Licensed growers compete against massive illegal supply, and OMMA agents are embedded in the criminal task force — administrative investigative powers are being used in prosecutions.

items.headline: OMMA New Testing Laboratory Rules Take Effect June 1, 2026
items.summary: OMMA's updated permanent testing laboratory rules took effect June 1, 2026, the product of a multi-year process directed by HB 4056 (2022) in which seven licensed lab directors met across 15 sessions (90+ hours) and submitted 120 pages of recommendations. The rules amend OAC 442:10-8-1 and OAC 442:10-8-3 and address manufacturer's instructions for use, enrichment steps and quality control sample requirements, inventory tag requirements, and clarifications on yeast/mold and potency-confirmation testing for distillate. A prior set of emergency rule updates for licensed laboratories was approved separately. Stakeholders at the public comment hearing had pressed OMMA for clearer standards and broader lab access to Metrc data.
items.whyItMatters: Cultivators and processors submitting products for third-party testing must now meet the updated standards. Products that fail under new methodology cannot legally enter the distribution chain. Labs out of compliance with the new equipment or procedural rules face potential OMMA enforcement, which could create bottlenecks affecting product release timelines for growers and manufacturers.

source.name: OMMA — New Testing Lab Rules Effective June 1

items.headline: Oklahoma Cannabis Market in Freefall — Wholesale Prices Down 63%, 6,000+ Grows Shuttered
items.summary: Oklahoma Watch and KGOU (NPR Oklahoma) reported June 2, 2026 that the state's medical cannabis market is in severe contraction. Wholesale prices have fallen from approximately $3,000/lb at peak to $1,100/lb (≈63% decline), making Oklahoma the national price floor at $2.11/gram. A 2023 study found licensed supply exceeded patient demand by roughly 64 to 1. At peak, Oklahoma had nearly 14,000 licensed businesses including ~9,400 growers; more than 6,000 grow locations have since shut down or been abandoned. The industry generated $670M+ in retail sales and $1.1B+ in wholesale transactions in 2025, contributing ~$47M in excise taxes and $60M+ in state and local sales taxes — but revenue and margins are collapsing. Governor Stitt is calling for voters to shut the program down entirely; legislative leadership has resisted abolition.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors face existential margin pressure from oversupply and falling wholesale prices at the same moment new DEA registration costs ($3,699/yr for manufacturers) are being imposed. Dispensary customers face the same squeeze from the retail side. The license moratorium extension (HB 3143, signed May 2026) prevents new entrants but does nothing to reduce the existing licensed supply overhang. CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers operate in this environment — the cost and compliance burden of DEA registration compounds an already-stressed market.

source.name: Oklahoma Watch — Growers Say Cannabis Bonanza Is Going to Pot

discourse.headline: Consultants marketing paid 'DEA registration' services
discourse.claim: Consulting firms and law practices are selling paid services to prepare/file DEA Schedule III registrations, framing the 7-section form as too complex to self-file; some imply a single SOP error will delay federal approval.
discourse.caution: The portal is direct-to-applicant with a $794 fee paid to DEA; there is no official statement that paid representation is required. Counsel may be prudent for the Section 4 liability question, but be skeptical of vendors implying only they can navigate a self-registration process.

discourse.headline: Section 4 'have you distributed without DEA registration?' debate
discourse.claim: The application asks whether owners/operators previously manufactured/distributed a controlled substance without DEA registration — which nearly every existing cannabis operator must answer 'yes' to. DEA publicly said the question is 'not intended as a categorical barrier.'
discourse.caution: DEA's reassurance is informal — not published policy or rulemaking, not binding. Former federal prosecutors have warned against treating it as legal protection. Independent counsel before submitting is essential.

discourse.headline: June 22 deadline confusion: WRONG for manufacturers/cultivators; NOW CONFIRMED as dispensary portal closing date by credible sources
discourse.claim: Named law firms (e.g., Fennemore Craig) and multiple cannabis outlets including MJBizDaily (June 5, 2026) cite June 22 as the registration deadline. New finding as of June 14: June 22 now appears to be the dispensary portal's own administrative closing date — distinct from the 60-day Federal Register window (June 27). For manufacturers and cultivators using Form 225, the correct window is June 26–27. Multiple unvetted sites (HealthDataConsortium.org, hrzenden.com/Zen Den Co.) continue to publish 'June 22' as the universal deadline.
discourse.caution: The situation has evolved: June 22 is NOT the general 60-day window deadline (June 26–27 is), but it IS now cited by MJBizDaily and Fennemore Craig as the dispensary-specific portal closing date. Oklahoma dispensaries should treat June 22 as their operative deadline. For CAKE as a cultivator/manufacturer, the operative deadline is June 26–27 via Form 225 — do not confuse the dispensary portal cutoff with the manufacturer window. The original explanation that June 22 is the ALJ hearing participant notification date (from the April 28 Federal Register) may also still apply; DEA appears to have used June 22 for multiple purposes in the same document. The source of the original confusion (conflating ALJ notification date with the registration deadline) has been compounded by the portal's own June 22 cutoff.

source.name: Fennemore Craig — 'Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026'

discourse.headline: '280E relief applies whether or not you register with DEA'
discourse.claim: OMMA guidance and several sources assert 280E relief applies to all state-licensed medical operators by virtue of Schedule III, regardless of DEA registration — used by some to justify skipping registration.
discourse.caution: IRS/Treasury has not issued formal guidance; OMMA's statement is its interpretation, not a binding ruling. Some tax attorneys dispute it. Acting on it before Treasury guidance carries material tax risk.

source.name: OMMA notice / Marijuana Policy Project Q&A

discourse.headline: Claims the whole rescheduling/registration framework could be voided
discourse.claim: The SAM/NDASA D.C. Circuit petition, if it gets a stay, could void the Final Order and potentially unwind registrations filed under it; commentators warn operators are 'racing through a door that could be slammed shut.'
discourse.caution: Active litigation — OMMA itself warned all information 'remains subject to change pending the lawsuit's outcome.' Factor this into any large federal-compliance capital spend.

discourse.headline: Vendors implying guaranteed or expedited DEA approvals
discourse.claim: Some marketing implies engaging a firm ensures approval or prevents delays; a Newswire PR item touts a vendor as having 'the answer' to DEA's registration questions.
discourse.caution: DEA makes registration decisions; no third party can guarantee outcomes or speed DEA's stated six-month review. Guarantees of approval or 'special access' are red flags. Paid PR is not verified reporting.

discourse.headline: 'DEA registration unlocks interstate commerce' — now being promoted by named companies via press release
discourse.claim: Some consultants suggest DEA registration will enable interstate cannabis commerce. This claim escalated in May 2026 when LEEF Brands (a California cannabis company) issued a May 8, 2026 press release titled 'LEEF Brands Files Applications for DEA Registration, Positioning for Interstate and Global Export Opportunities,' framing registration as the gateway to selling across state lines and exporting internationally. The release was picked up by Cannabis Business Times.
discourse.caution: Interstate cannabis commerce is not authorized by the Final Order, the Federal Register rules, or any federal guidance. The DOJ order explicitly states registered operators remain 'subject to the limitations of their state license.' The treaty conflict with the 1961 Single Convention further blocks export pathways. LEEF Brands' release uses future-conditional language ('as regulatory frameworks evolve'), but the headline framing has been treated as current fact in trade coverage. Do not make Oklahoma business decisions premised on interstate commerce unlocking via DEA registration.

discourse.headline: Oklahoma operators confused over OMMA 'optional' vs OBNDD 'required'
discourse.claim: Local Oklahoma coverage and industry media show operators unsure whether to register now or wait for the manufacturer portal, and confused that OMMA calls dispensary registration optional while OBNDD calls manufacturer/distributor registration mandatory.
discourse.caution: These are different agencies governing different license types — not contradictory. Industry media is not a substitute for counsel. Much of the live conversation is in private Facebook/Slack operator groups not publicly indexed.

discourse.headline: Operators warn: DEA registration is 'giving DEA a roadmap' to your operation
discourse.claim: Some industry veterans argue DEA registration creates permanent operational exposure beyond the Section 4 self-incrimination question — it documents facility location, scale, personnel with controlled-substance access, and supply-chain partners, and grants DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority. The concern is that previously extralegal operations are now fully mapped for federal enforcement.
discourse.caution: DEA's on-demand inspection authority is a statutory consequence of Schedule III registration under 21 U.S.C. 880, not unique to cannabis. Operators who fear this 'roadmap' risk should weigh it against the concrete risks of non-registration: OBNDD sanctions, loss of expedited safe-harbor, and potential license jeopardy. This is a legitimate risk factor for counsel to evaluate — not a verified basis for refusing to register.

source.name: Beard Bros Pharms — Oklahoma registration concerns

discourse.headline: MMJ International press campaign claims June 29 hearing unconstitutional — 'DELAYED AGAIN!'; hearing remains on schedule
discourse.claim: Since May 18, 2026, MMJ International Holdings has run an escalating public press campaign with alarming headlines: 'DELAYED AGAIN! Will Marijuana Rescheduling Survive Constitutional Challenges?' (May 26); 'DEA's Last Marijuana Gamble: Unconstitutional ALJ System Collapses'; and others via AccessNewswire and pressrelease.com. MMJ formally joined the D.C. Circuit challenge (cases 26-1106/1130/1136) on May 29, and on June 9 co-filed the stay motion. MMJ has also announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the June 29 hearing itself on constitutional grounds (DOJ admitted DEA ALJs' removal protections are unconstitutional in MMJ BioPharma v. Bondi, Feb. 27, 2025). As of June 14, 2026, no court has granted any injunction or stay, and the hearing remains on schedule.
discourse.caution: MMJ International is an interested litigation party — a pharmaceutical company that spent 7+ years pursuing FDA/DEA approval channels and now competes against newly-Schedule-III state-licensed operators. Its press campaign is advocacy, not journalism. The constitutional issue over DEA ALJ removal protections is real (DOJ conceded it), but it primarily affects the broader rescheduling hearing, not the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect. Treat any claim that the June 29 hearing 'has been cancelled' or 'will definitely not proceed' as unverified until confirmed by the DEA or a federal court order. The risk of Oklahoma cultivators/processors not registering before June 27 due to MMJ's 'framework may be voided' narrative is concrete and immediate; the stay risk, while real, has not materialized.

source.name: PharmiWeb — June 29 Hearing Faces Constitutional Issues (May 18, 2026)

discourse.headline: Former federal prosecutor and IRS attorney warn: Do Not Rush DEA Registration
discourse.claim: A former federal prosecutor and a former IRS attorney are publicly advising cannabis operators NOT to file DEA registration before the June 27 expedited deadline without first consulting specialized criminal-defense and tax counsel. Arguments: (1) the Section 4 prior-distribution question documents conduct that was federally illegal when it occurred, and DEA's informal 'not a categorical barrier' statement is not binding policy; (2) the D.C. Circuit challenges could stay or vacate the entire order, making rushed registrations moot; (3) for operators whose primary concern is state-license continuity rather than federal supply-chain expansion, the registration risk may outweigh the benefit.
discourse.caution: This advice conflicts with OBNDD Director Anderson's directive that ALL Oklahoma OMMA manufacturers and distributors MUST register by ~June 27 to avoid OBNDD administrative sanctions up to license revocation. Unlike dispensaries (for whom OMMA calls registration optional), Oklahoma cultivators and processors face a state-level mandate from OBNDD — not merely a federal opportunity. Operators following this national commentary without accounting for Oklahoma's OBNDD requirement risk losing their OBNDD manufacturer registration and by extension their OMMA grower/processor license. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel familiar with both DEA registration and OBNDD requirements before deciding.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — Former Prosecutors Warn Against Rushing

discourse.headline: 'Federal rescheduling rewards a cannabis supply chain built on fraudulent safety testing' — MMJ International press campaign
discourse.claim: MMJ International Holdings (press release via AccessNewswire, June 3, 2026) claims the April 28 rescheduling order granted Schedule III status to a supply chain characterized by 'documented lab fraud, contaminated products, and a near-total absence of safety science' — without requiring a single state-licensed product to demonstrate safety, purity, or clinical efficacy. The company calls on federal courts and Congress to scrutinize the state-licensed supply chain before extending federal legitimacy. MMJ International is simultaneously a D.C. Circuit petitioner in Case 26-1136 and a co-filer of the June 9 stay motion.
discourse.caution: MMJ International Holdings is an interested litigation party — this is paid-wire advocacy, not independent journalism. Oklahoma has had documented lab-fraud cases (OMMA has acted on testing violations and issued product recalls involving fraudulent or failed lab results), so the underlying concern about testing integrity is not fabricated; however, the framing is designed to advance MMJ International's litigation strategy and commercial interests. As of June 14, no court has accepted this argument as grounds for a stay or vacatur. Oklahoma operators should monitor whether this argument gains traction in the D.C. Circuit, as a ruling that imposed additional safety-testing conditions on DEA registration would directly affect cultivators and manufacturers.

discourse.headline: Unvetted content-marketing websites publishing authoritative-sounding but unreliable DEA compliance guidance
discourse.claim: Multiple websites with no identifiable editorial standards, named authors, or organizational credentials are publishing DEA registration guidance that appears in Google results alongside legitimate law-firm content: HealthDataConsortium.org ('June 22, 2026: What Medical Cannabis Operators Must Do Now' — no author, no organizational affiliation found); hrzenden.com / Zen Den Co. ('DEA Registration Deadline Cannabis June 22' — apparent lead-generation content from a non-cannabis business); WeedPress 'The Paper Trail' (aggressive compliance-cliff framing, operated by an individual, targeting South Dakota operators but indexed broadly). These sites are amplifying the June 22 deadline confusion and creating additional noise for operators seeking reliable guidance.
discourse.caution: Oklahoma operators searching for DEA registration guidance are encountering these sites in search results, sometimes above credible law-firm or government content. Their articles may contain factual errors, be out of date, or be designed primarily for lead generation rather than compliance accuracy. No website is an authoritative source unless it ends in .gov. Cross-check any guidance against primary sources: the Federal Register notice (federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/28/2026-08177), the DEA Diversion Control page (deadiversion.usdoj.gov), OMMA official bulletins (oklahoma.gov/omma), and OBNDD's director bulletin (obndd.ok.gov).

source.name: HealthDataConsortium.org — 'June 22, 2026: What Medical Cannabis Operators Must Do Now'
